sentences.text: My name is Yerucham.
No, right?
Yerucham Goose.
That's in English.
In Polish, Guz.
1919. 20th of December. 1919, the 20th of December.
Berezne.
Berezne is [?
Wolyn ?] on-- the province is Rivne.
Rivne.
About 3,500, 4,000 Jewish people.
All in the city were Jewish people.
In the heart of the city was not Jewish.
Ukraine is [INAUDIBLE].
There's Ukraine Polish.
It was called the Ukraine, got a part.
In the city, either Polish, the Jewish people, and then was the army.
The Polish army in the city, too.
Not too many what I know.
Not the city, the villages.
Farmers, like, say this.
Heart of the city is not farmers.
It's not that big city, you know.
Well, the Jewish community-- about religion, or say about some other things?
The Jewish people-- there was the most business people in the city.
About 70% business people, 80%, and 20% workers.
And then, the most people, the Jewish people was more religion.
Come Friday night, Friday after 4 or 5 o'clock, the whole store is closed.
And when you want to pay $10 [INAUDIBLE] something to buy, you couldn't find that.
And everybody went to shul.
And then Saturday, the same thing as Friday night.
And Saturday, the same thing.
Close every store.
Because the whole city was Jewish people.
And what did your family do?
My family used to be butchers.
My father's a butcher, and I got in two brothers.
Well, I would say one got a butcher shop, and got a store to sell meat, kosher meat.
And one brother just bought cattle and passed them to my parents.
Until my brother sold them and made profit on them.
How many people were there in your family?
My family was two, three-- one, two, and father's three, mother's four, and my sister's five.
The name is-- my father is Mottel Guz, Gnesha.
And then was Gittel.
No, Gnesha was my mother.
And the sister was Gittel.
Then it was my sister's kids, too, grandchildren, what my mother used to have.
There's a lot of them.
I couldn't tell you the name correctly of them.
No, I'm sorry.
Not my brothers, Not their names.
The youngest.
Well, just like usual.
In Berezne, the kids, they put them before they're grown up to six, seven years, they get in a cheder, in a rabbi's special room.
They used to-- then, later we got a Talmud Torah for grown-ups.
And I was grown up, then I went later to Talmud Torah.
And then I went to the Polish school after that.
I remember a little bit.
I remember that I couldn't walk myself.
My mother used to walk me over to the cheder.
There was one room.
But the rabbi-- and he got about 10 kids.
And he was teaching the kids.
Well, I would have got-- I liked-- like they say now-- they say now, it's soccer, I used to like.
And tennis.
I used to play tennis other way, you know.
That's what we used to play, all of us.
Well, I remember, just I couldn't remember too much the names.
I couldn't-- I don't remember them.
You see, after [? reparation, ?] I don't remember so much like I used to be.
Well, the Jewish holidays was, come Friday night, which is [INAUDIBLE].
Or Rosh Hashanah night.
Friday nights, father used to go in the shul, [INAUDIBLE] the prayer.
If the case was a few poor people, what they got no food, no home.
He took to meet them, the hungry people, and brought them to the house.
Friday night, and they served the dinner.
He was between us, serving dinner.
And the same Saturday morning.
Anybody was there, the same thing.
Anybody was hungry, he took them home after dinner time, and they served them dinner.
Then, later afternoon, there was [INAUDIBLE].
Maybe you know that.
Then used to come the [INAUDIBLE] in the synagogue, and pick up food for the [INAUDIBLE].
Passover.
Well, Passover is the famous holiday because then, they went to the-- they bought some material.
They went to the tailor, and they made a suit up.
And got a shoemaker and made some shoes up.
And it was interesting.
Then, when we went to the synagogue, I was looking to the shoes.
They were shiny, see if anybody looks at them.
I was so excited for the Passover.
This the only time what we all dressed up new everything.
Yes.
We, in our town, there was a-- then, what the Talmud Torah what I tell you, they used to have a basement in this building.
And they built up ovens.
And three, four people was in the oven, baking the matzohs.
And then we used to buy them by the pound.
They put in a big box on the shoulders.
And two people carry the matzohs to the house.
In Berezne, on [?
Cupernica, ?] the main street.
And we got-- we used to have a big house, two stories with a balcony.
It was the only house in Berezne, we got this kind of house.
In this house, we got about, what, maybe about 12, 14 rooms.
Yes, we got a steady girl.
And some comes and help Ma too.
And the laundry was sent out, to wash the laundry.
Yes, it was comfortably off.
Because we got the butcher shop.
And then we used to supply meat for the army.
Every-- we used to take a contract, let's say, for a year.
There was an auction.
And my father paid the biggest-- the lowest price to deliver the meat.
And then he got the auction to give beef to the army.
Well, he was active in this-- [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] that there was.
You know what is a [INAUDIBLE].
In this event for collection for poor people, for supporting people, people you know.
Mother used to work in the store, by the meat.
She used to sell the meat.
My father used to-- help him, too, before he bought the cattle and the farms, he was at market.
And then got them to the slaughterhouse.
And then in the morning, we brought the meat over to the store.
He carved them in pieces.
He prepared, and mother used to sell it.
Pardon?
I used to have a sister, Gittel, that I mentioned.
She took care of me, when I was little, it was.
Yes.
There [INAUDIBLE], not in my city.
Jews-- in '39, it was known that in Rivne, the big town, they wouldn't let in Jewish kids for university, you know, when they have to go on far for school.
It was just public school.
In bigger schools, they was against them.
Not what I remember.
Because the most anti-Semitism was given by the Polish people.
The Ukraine, and the farmers, were the worst.
They never know so much about what goes on in the world.
They know just in the morning, wake up 5 o'clock, go out in the field, and the cattle.
And that's all they know.
They don't know nothing.
Anything, nothing about what goes on in the world.
In fact, we, in the bigger town, don't know either.
Because radio is not-- it was not radios, and it was not television.
And our paper used to come in once in a month.
If you were in Rivne, delivered papers, a newspaper.
And that's all we know what goes on in the world.
Yes, we got electricity.
In fact, we once made a collection for the poor people, the [INAUDIBLE] committee.
You know what a [INAUDIBLE] committee.
A [INAUDIBLE] committee means when some-- no parents, just kids alone.
It was a group what they made some [INAUDIBLE].
And sometimes they tried to show some radio.
And we used to have a big building, in the rented-- not rented, they give us a big room.
And they start showing the radios.
And [INAUDIBLE] we couldn't understand not a word.
Between time it was common for 20 kids, 30 kids, and listen that.
Jewish.
Yiddish.
Polish, of course.
It was Poland then.
I spoke Polish, because I was-- myself was selling meat, too.
When we got the army, I used to prepare the meat for them.
Well, since six year, because everybody's Polish, they're speaking.
Yeah, then we'd gotten better.
Better Polish.
Not what I remember.
Not-- because it was a small school.
And I don't think [INAUDIBLE].
Because I was between the smaller kids, six, seven-year-olds.
Yes, I was.
I think I tell you last time.
I forgot already what it was.
Yes.
Hashomer Hatzair.
In Gordonia.
I was there twice.
See [INAUDIBLE].
In two place.
First, I was in Gordonia.
Then there was not enough kids, and I went to Hashomer Hatzair.
Well, they was telling us about Israel, and telling us we have to buy some trees.
It make the Germans [INAUDIBLE] singing.
And then we'd make some visits from one town to the other town, just like, you know.
You make some-- in the evening [? expire ?] in the woods.
So fathers in the evening, it was come together four, five cities, together with the [INAUDIBLE].
Then we enjoy it together..
I never think what I'm going to do.
Then, I think we were 14 year olds.
After my bar mitzvah.
My bar mitzvah was not very big like in Canada.
My bar mitzvah, when I was 13 years, my dad went to the market and bought a small bottle whiskey.
And my mother make the cake up overnight.
And the morning, we went to the synagogue.
And that was the bar mitzvah.
After, we went to the work.
Comes on Saturday, we don't work.
But through the week, we went to work.
I was helping my-- my parents were butcher.
Well, I just-- between them, I learned some.
Between the parents.
Well, I used to have a few girls.
Go out with friends.
Well, we used to dance in the organization, Hashomer and--
Boys and girls together, because not was-- Hashomer was not a religion, you know that.
They danced together.
And some they make speeches.
Some-- everybody give a speech, what he thinks and what [INAUDIBLE], what they like to be.
You know, that's the world.
Yes.
Well, I-- not that I remember.
I remember [INAUDIBLE] used to go around in my town on the-- in this time.
And there was-- to every house, they went and they chopped the wood.
And they saved up money to go to Israel.
The truth, I tell you, I don't remember.
No, we just find out later.
When the Russian come in to us, then the Russian tell us, this one is Germany, but they going to war together.
There was already rumors.
But not to radio.
We haven't got no radio, no paper.
Nobody has.
The only thing we find out is when the Russians were walking to us already.
Then we know the war is on.
The other way, we don't know that.
At least, I don't know that.
And I don't think nobody knew.
I was young then.
I think nobody knew that.
Well, they changed a lot.
You see, when you got a clothing store, they went in, they broke down the locks, and took everything out from the clothing store.
And they was very poor.
It was nightgowns.
It was girls with them.
They dressed up in nightgowns for dress.
They never say what's a nightgown, what's a dress.
That's how poor they was.
And the rich ones, very rich, they send them out to Siberia.
The normal, you know, they just took out everything what they got.
And they start trying to look for them for work.
My father's butcher shop, beginning, they let us work.
And later, they say, you know what, we let you have the store.
You could go on.
Just they put in so much taxes, you have to close them down as soon as possible, you couldn't exist.
And that's sad.
Well, I don't know too much about it, because with the rebbe, I don't got connections.
They got my parents, my grandpa used to go to the rebbe.
I was not too much with the rebbe for to see the rebbe.
That's why I don't got too much recollection.
I was-- you know, kids have their own [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] with this, with that.
We never got what the rabbi said.
And I'm sure the rabbi don't know too much either.
When he don't have a newspaper, he don't have radio, what anybody would know about it?
Do you remember your zaide?
My zaide?
No.
Yeah.
I was born later, seven, eight years later.
In fact, my name is after my zaide.
I think about two years.
And then the Germans-- until they broke up with Germany.
Before they was with Germany together.
The war, decided Stalin, he was going to take Poland.
And the Germans were going to take the other part.
And after-- I think six months later, the Germans start going [INAUDIBLE] everything.
And the Russians tell us, come with us.
Because the Germans are going to kill you.
They're going to kill everybody.
And my dad used to say, I am not a politician.
I never got business with politics.
Why they should kill me?
In fact, they've got a nice, big home, and make a nice living.
And he was happy to show everybody that he didn't want to run away.
Because a lot ran away.
They was connected with something.
With what?
With-- maybe with-- I don't know.
But he was afraid for some politics, not for the Germans.
For other things.
He was with the Russians, let's say.
When the Russians run away, they went with the Russians.
Well, with me, it's the same thing.
I was more with my parents.
When the parents said we don't go, we're staying here, then I accept his word.
OK.
Then, when the Germans came in and start fighting, take over Berezne, then we decided-- we used to have a big house, a two story-- to go over to my brother across the road.
He got a small house.
Everybody's going to sit with my brother.
Kieva.
There was a Kieva.
Then-- OK, go on?
Then, soon everybody left to my brother.
And maybe two hours later, they were shooting-- the Germans was fighting together.
And they put in a bomb right in this house what I was, the whole family.
They was killing a few friends of my family.
And I was crippled too, then.
They couldn't find me.
After, they-- this one's crippled.
They took him out.
And they couldn't find-- they didn't know where I am.
And they start looking for me.
And they find that I was under the gravel when the house-- then they looking.
They find some-- I used to wear boots, leather boots.
And they see the boots, and they pulled me out.
And they brought me over.
Then about 10 miles from [INAUDIBLE], there was a village.
And I was there for three, four days and don't know what goes on with me.
